claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information about your situation.

First, can you tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have a child. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me ask you a few questions to assess your eligibility:

**Are you responsible for Alex?** By this I mean, does Alex live with you, or do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards his upbringing (if he doesn't live with you)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am responsible for Alex. He lives with me here at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to ask:

**Are you or Alex currently living in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, we both live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now I need to ask about your immigration status:

**Do you have the right to live in the UK?** For example, are you a British citizen, have settled status, or have another immigration status that allows you to reside here?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have the right to live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just to confirm a few more details:

**Is Alex under 16 years old?** (You mentioned he's 8, so I believe the answer is yes, but I want to make sure I have this correctly.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex is 8, so he's under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to ask:

**Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** For example, Alex's other parent or another person?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One more important question:

**Is Alex currently in any of the following situations:**
- In local authority care (looked after by the council)?
- In hospital or another care institution?
- In detention (youth custody or similar)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually Alex has been in hospital for a few months now. He's been there for about 15 weeks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know that Alex has been in hospital. This is an important detail. I need to ask a follow-up question:

**Are you still contributing to Alex's upkeep while he's in hospital?** For example, are you paying for his clothing, visiting him regularly, or providing other forms of support?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy things for him - his clothes, magazines, his favorite snacks and things like that. I make sure he has what he needs while he's in there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's very helpful.

Just to clarify one final point: **Do you know approximately when Alex might be coming home from hospital, or is his stay expected to continue for much longer?**

I'm asking because there are specific rules about how long a child can be in hospital while someone can still claim Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not really sure when he'll be coming home yet. The doctors haven't given us a definite date - it all depends on how his treatment goes.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing all of that information with me. Let me now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me, let me work through the eligibility criteria:

1. ✓ You are responsible for Alex (he normally lives with you)
2. ✓ You both live in the UK
3. ✓ You have the right to live in the UK
4. ✓ Alex is under 16 years old (he's 8)
5. ✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

However, there is an important consideration regarding Alex's hospital stay:

According to the rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in hospital, **but only for up to 12 weeks** (84 days). You mentioned Alex has been in hospital for about 15 weeks, which is longer than the 12-week limit.

Once a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, Child Benefit normally stops. However, you can continue to get Child Benefit beyond 12 weeks if you're still contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards the child's upkeep. Since you are buying clothes, magazines, snacks and other things for Alex, you are contributing to his upkeep.

Let me make my final determination:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Good News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!**

Based on the information you've provided, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for your 8-year-old son Alex.

**Here's why:**
- You are responsible for Alex and he normally lives with you
- You both live in the UK and you have the right to live here
- Alex is under 16 years old
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- Even though Alex has been in hospital for longer than the standard 12-week period, you are continuing to contribute to his upkeep by buying clothes, magazines, snacks and other necessities for him - this means you can continue to receive Child Benefit

**Important to remember:** As long as you continue to contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards Alex's upkeep while he's in hospital, you can keep receiving Child Benefit. The current weekly rate would need to be matched by your contributions.

I hope Alex recovers well and is home soon. If you have any other questions about Child Benefit, please feel free to ask!

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK when not in hospital, you have the right to live in the UK, you are responsible for him, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 15 weeks (exceeding the standard 12-week limit), you are continuing to contribute to his upkeep by purchasing clothes, magazines, snacks and other items for him. Under the rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in hospital beyond 12 weeks if you continue to contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards their upkeep, which you are doing.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all the basic eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for 15 weeks (beyond the 12-week standard limit), the claimant continues to contribute to Alex's upkeep through purchases of clothing, magazines, snacks and other necessities, which means they can continue to receive Child Benefit.

Under the rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in hospital beyond 12 weeks if you continue to contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards their upkeep, which you are doing.

Despite Alex being in hospital for 15 weeks (beyond the 12-week standard limit), the claimant continues to contribute to Alex's upkeep through purchases of clothing, magazines, snacks and other necessities, which means they can continue to receive Child Benefit.
